questions.question: Define a forest in data structures and explain how it differs from a tree.
keyPoints: A forest is a collection of disjoint trees
Trees in a forest do not share any vertices or edges
A tree is a special case of forest with exactly one component
Removing root of a tree gives a forest of subtrees

questions.question: Explain the left-child right-sibling representation of a forest.
keyPoints: Each node has two pointers: left-child and right-sibling
Left-child points to first child
Right-sibling points to next sibling node
Converts general tree to binary tree representation

questions.question: What is a spanning forest? How many edges does a spanning forest have if there are n vertices and c connected components?
keyPoints: Spanning forest includes all vertices of the graph
It is a forest (acyclic) subgraph
Number of edges = n - c
For connected graph (c=1), edges = n-1 (spanning tree)

questions.question: Explain how a forest can be represented using parent pointers.
keyPoints: Use an array where each index represents a node
Each element stores index of its parent
Root nodes have parent = -1 or NULL
Multiple -1 values indicate multiple trees (forest)

questions.question: What is the relationship between a tree and a forest? Explain with an example.
keyPoints: A tree is a connected acyclic graph
A forest is a collection of disconnected trees
A tree is a special case of forest (with one component)
Removing root of tree creates forest of subtrees

questions.question: Define: (i) Rooted Forest (ii) Ordered Forest (iii) k-ary Forest
keyPoints: Rooted Forest: Each tree has designated root, every node has one parent except roots
Ordered Forest: Trees and children have specific ordering
k-ary Forest: Collection of k-ary trees (max k children per node) that are disjoint

questions.question: What are the applications of forests in computer science?
keyPoints: Representing hierarchical file systems
Disjoint set data structures (union-find)
Representing multiple expression parse trees
Graph algorithms like finding connected components

questions.question: Explain the concept of a forest in data structures with examples. Discuss how forests are represented in memory and explain the various types of forests.
keyPoints: Definition: Collection of disjoint trees
Example: Multiple separate tree structures
Representation: Parent pointer array, Left-child right-sibling
Types: Rooted, Ordered, k-ary, General forest
Properties: Disjoint, acyclic, n-c edges formula

questions.question: Explain the relationship between trees and forests. How can a tree be converted to a forest and vice versa? Discuss the representation techniques used for forests.
keyPoints: Tree is connected acyclic graph, forest is collection of such trees
Removing root of tree gives forest of subtrees
Forest can be merged by adding a super-root
Parent pointer representation
Left-child right-sibling representation
Adjacency list/matrix representation

questions.question: Discuss the graph theory perspective of forests. What is a spanning forest? Explain how you would find the number of connected components in a graph using forest concepts.
keyPoints: In graph theory, forest is acyclic graph
Each component is a tree
Spanning forest includes all vertices
Edges in spanning forest = n - c
Use DFS/BFS to find connected components
Apply Union-Find data structure

questions.question: Design algorithms for the following operations on a forest represented using parent pointers: (i) Finding all roots (ii) Finding the depth of any node (iii) Counting number of trees in the forest.
keyPoints: Finding roots: Scan parent array, nodes with parent=-1 are roots
Finding depth: Follow parent pointers from node to root, count steps
Counting trees: Count number of roots (nodes with parent=-1)
Time complexity analysis for each operation
Space complexity considerations
